skill.name: lark-openapi-explorer
skill.description: Explore native Feishu and Lark OpenAPI documentation when existing lark skills or registered CLI commands do not cover a task.
skill.summary: Find native Lark OpenAPI endpoints and prepare safe lark-cli API calls.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backticks, URLs, path traversal strings, weak crypto, and entropy indicators in SKILL.md. Review found the backticks are markdown code spans or command examples, the URLs point to official Lark documentation, the traversal strings are relative documentation links, and the entropy findings are caused by Chinese text. The confirmed risk is medium because the skill instructs agents to use lark-cli against live Lark APIs, including mutating methods, although it includes confirmation rules for write and delete actions.

medium_findings.title: Native Lark API Calls Can Modify Workspace Data
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs use of lark-cli api for GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE requests. This is legitimate for an OpenAPI explorer, but mutating calls can change live Lark workspace data if an agent acts without clear user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples explicitly include mutating HTTP methods and live workspace operations. The risk is moderated by documented confirmation and dry-run guidance.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick findings are markdown code fences, inline code spans, or documented command examples. No Ruby source code or executable backtick operator was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is markdown, and each occurrence is documentation syntax or a displayed lark-cli example. There is no Ruby execution context.

low_findings.title: Official Documentation URLs Are Expected Network References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to official Feishu and Lark OpenAPI documentation. They are needed for the skill purpose and do not show credential exfiltration or untrusted endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains match the documented Feishu and Lark OpenAPI documentation sites. No evidence found of sending secrets or user data to an unrelated endpoint.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alerts Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are relative markdown links to sibling skills and an ellipsis in a documentation URL example. No filesystem read, write, or path construction behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown references, not runtime file operations. No evidence found of user-controlled paths reaching filesystem APIs.

low_findings.title: Crypto And Entropy Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak crypto and high entropy findings point to front matter and Chinese description text. No cryptographic implementation, encoded payload, or binary content was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 4 is a natural-language skill description containing Chinese characters and OpenAPI terminology. The surrounding file is readable markdown with no encoded payload.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mutating lark-cli API Methods
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented command pattern includes POST, PUT, DELETE, and PATCH examples through lark-cli api. These methods can modify external Lark state and require explicit user confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly documents write and delete API methods and sensitive-operation safeguards. This confirms a real operational risk without showing malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Teams often need Lark actions that are not wrapped by existing skills or CLI shortcuts. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through official OpenAPI docs and prepares safe lark-cli calls.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether an existing lark skill or lark-cli command already covers the request.
Uses the Feishu or Lark llms.txt index to locate relevant OpenAPI documentation.
Extracts API method, path, parameters, permissions, response fields, and error codes from official docs.
Prepares lark-cli api guidance for GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, and PATCH requests.
Requires confirmation before write or delete API calls and recommends dry-run previews when available.
limitations: Requires lark-cli to be installed and authenticated before API calls can work.
Depends on official documentation being available and accurate.
Does not replace permission review for production workspace changes.
Documentation is primarily Chinese and may require translation for English users.

use_cases.title: Find an Unwrapped API
use_cases.description: Locate the native OpenAPI endpoint when a requested Lark action is missing from current CLI commands.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Controlled Workspace Change
use_cases.description: Identify required permissions and request details before changing chats, members, or announcements.

use_cases.title: Build Internal Automation
use_cases.description: Convert official OpenAPI documentation into repeatable lark-cli call guidance for team workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Check API Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether lark-cli already supports my Lark task. If not, find the relevant native OpenAPI documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Find Read Endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the official Feishu or Lark OpenAPI endpoint for reading this information, including method, path, parameters, and scopes.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare CLI Call
prompt_templates.prompt: Use official Lark OpenAPI documentation to prepare a lark-cli api call plan. Include required permissions and common errors.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Safe Write Operation
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the native OpenAPI needed for this workspace change, explain the impact, and ask for confirmation before any write call.

output_examples.input: I need to add two users to a Lark group, but the CLI command is missing.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies the chat member add endpoint, lists required member identifiers and permissions, and asks for confirmation before preparing the write call.

output_examples.input: Find how to update a group announcement through Lark OpenAPI.
output_examples.output: The skill checks existing IM commands, locates the announcement update documentation, summarizes required fields, and warns that the call changes live chat content.

output_examples.input: Show me the read-only API for checking a Lark resource status.
output_examples.output: The skill locates the relevant documentation, reports the read method, required query parameters, response fields, and likely permission scopes.

best_practices: Confirm existing lark-cli support before exploring native OpenAPI documentation.
Use official Feishu or Lark documentation as the source for every method, path, and parameter.
Ask for explicit approval before POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE operations.
anti_patterns: Do not guess API paths or request fields from memory.
Do not run mutating API calls without user confirmation.
Do not expose tokens, tenant credentials, or personal identifiers in shared output.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps find native Feishu or Lark OpenAPI endpoints when existing skills or CLI commands do not cover a task.

faq.question: Does it call APIs automatically?
faq.answer: It provides guidance for lark-cli api calls. Write and delete operations require explicit confirmation.

faq.question: Which documentation sources does it use?
faq.answer: It uses the official Feishu and Lark OpenAPI llms.txt documentation indexes and linked API documents.

faq.question: Can it work in English?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill instructs the assistant to translate Chinese documentation content when the user works in English.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: The local lark-cli tool must be available, and the user must have valid authentication for API calls.

faq.question: Is this safe for production workspaces?
faq.answer: Use caution. Read-only calls are lower risk, but write and delete calls can change live workspace data.
